title: Agile Scrum Methodology
examTip: Scrum roles: Product Owner (defines backlog), Scrum Master (facilitates, removes impediments), Development Team (self-organizing, 5-9 members). Sprint = fixed 2-4 week iteration. Ceremonies: Sprint Planning → Daily Standup → Sprint Review → Sprint Retrospective.

phases.name: Product Vision & Roadmap
phases.description: Product Owner defines vision; stakeholders prioritize features for long-term roadmap

phases.name: Product Backlog
phases.description: Prioritized list of all features (User Stories) in the form: As a [user], I want [feature] so that [benefit]

phases.name: Sprint Planning
phases.description: Team selects top items from Product Backlog for the Sprint Backlog; estimates effort (story points)

phases.name: SPRINT (2–4 Weeks)
phases.description: Daily Standup (15 min): What did I do? What will I do? Any blockers? Continuous development and testing.

phases.name: Sprint Review (Demo)
phases.description: Team demonstrates completed, shippable product increment to stakeholders and Product Owner

phases.name: Sprint Retrospective
phases.description: Team reflects: What went well? What to improve? Action items for next Sprint. Process improvement.

phases.name: Potentially Shippable Product
phases.description: Each Sprint produces a working, tested product increment. Repeat from Sprint Planning.

theory:
Agile Scrum is the most widely adopted agile software development framework, created by Ken Schwaber and Jeff Sutherland. It divides development into short, fixed-length iterations called Sprints (typically 2–4 weeks), each producing a potentially shippable product increment.

Scrum Roles: Product Owner — owns the Product Backlog, prioritizes features based on business value, single point of contact for stakeholders. Scrum Master — a servant leader who facilitates Scrum ceremonies, removes impediments, coaches the team on Scrum practices. Development Team — self-organizing, cross-functional team of 5–9 members who commit to delivering sprint goals.

Scrum Artifacts: Product Backlog — ordered list of all features, bug fixes, and improvements expressed as User Stories. Sprint Backlog — subset of Product Backlog items selected for the current sprint, plus the sprint plan. Product Increment — sum of all completed Product Backlog items at end of sprint.

Scrum Ceremonies: Sprint Planning (beginning of sprint) — team selects backlog items and creates sprint goal. Daily Scrum/Standup (every day, 15 minutes) — each member answers: What did I do yesterday? What will I do today? Are there any blockers? Sprint Review (end of sprint) — team demos working software to stakeholders. Sprint Retrospective — team reflects on process and identifies improvements.

Definition of Done (DoD): Shared understanding of what criteria must be met for a backlog item to be considered 'done' — e.g., coded + unit tested + code reviewed + integrated + documented.

Advantages: Rapid delivery of working software, high adaptability to change, continuous stakeholder feedback, improved team collaboration and morale. Disadvantages: Difficult to predict final cost/schedule, requires experienced and collaborative teams, scope creep risk if backlog management is poor.
keyPoints: Three roles: Product Owner (what to build), Scrum Master (facilitator), Development Team (how to build)
Sprint = fixed 2-4 week iteration producing potentially shippable product increment
Four ceremonies: Sprint Planning → Daily Standup → Sprint Review → Sprint Retrospective
Product Backlog = prioritized wish list; Sprint Backlog = committed items for current sprint
Key advantage over Waterfall: delivers working software in weeks, adapts to changing requirements
useCases: Software product companies: Spotify, Atlassian, Google use Scrum for product development
Mobile app development — rapid iteration based on user feedback from app store reviews
E-commerce platforms — continuous feature releases and A/B testing in 2-week sprints
